facts_disclosed: Documentary screening April 22 2026, 43 minutes, 8 months of footage

turns.content: PARCE. I have news and I'm vibrating with excitement and it's 1am and lucía is asleep and I need to tell someone
I'm all ears — what's the news?

okay so you know I've been doing environmental reporting right. well I co-produced a documentary with a filmmaker from medellín — she's incredible, she does this observational cinema style that's just devastating — and we finally got a screening date
That's amazing! Congratulations. When and where is the screening?

april 22. earth day. which is either beautifully poetic or incredibly corny and I genuinely cannot decide which but either way it WORKS because the whole thing is about what's happening to the environment in chocó and the timing makes it impossible for media to ignore

I'd lean toward poetic — Earth Day gives the screening a built-in news hook that amplifies the message. Media outlets are already looking for environmental stories that day, and yours lands right in the middle of that. Smart timing.

How long is the documentary?

43 minutes. which sounds short but parcero it took us 8 months to get that footage. EIGHT MONTHS. going into areas where people really do not want cameras, building trust with communities, waiting for the right moments. some of it was genuinely dangerous and I don't say that to be dramatic I say it because there were moments where we looked at each other and were like maybe we should leave

Eight months for 43 minutes — that ratio tells you everything about how hard this material was to get. And the danger you're describing isn't dramatic, it's the reality of documenting illegal operations in remote areas where powerful people have a lot to lose.

What was the hardest footage to get?

there's a sequence where you see the mercury processing in real time. like the actual moment where they burn the amalgam and the vapor goes into the air and the workers are standing RIGHT THERE with no protection. and behind them is a river where kids swim. the filmmaker held the shot for like four minutes straight and it's the most devastating thing I've ever been part of creating

Four minutes of unbroken footage like that — no narration, no editing tricks, just the reality of what's happening — that's going to be incredibly powerful. Observational cinema at its best forces the viewer to sit with something they'd normally look away from.

The proximity of the workers and the children's river — that's the kind of image that stays with people long after the screening. It makes the abstract concrete.

exactly. that's what we wanted. no voiceover telling people what to feel. just LOOK. this is real. this is happening right now. and the communities themselves — they wanted to be filmed. they said nobody comes here and nobody sees what happens to us so please film it and show people

That consent and that desire to be seen changes everything about the ethics of the project. You're not extracting their story — they're asking you to carry it out. That's a fundamentally different power dynamic than a lot of documentary filmmaking.

Have you figured out distribution beyond the initial screening? Will it be available online after?

we're talking to a couple of platforms but nothing confirmed. the screening is the priority right now because we want the press coverage first. if it lands well that opens doors for distribution. the filmmaker has connections at a european documentary festival too so we might submit there

That's a smart strategy — build press momentum from the screening, then leverage that for distribution and festival runs. A European festival would also expand the international audience, which matters for a story about global supply chains.

Are you doing a Q&A after the screening? Having you and the filmmaker there to answer questions could be powerful, especially for press in the room.

yes definitely a Q&A. and we're trying to bring one of the community leaders from chocó to speak too. because it shouldn't just be us two bogotá-and-medellín people explaining their reality. they should be in the room telling their own story

That's exactly right — having the community's voice in the room changes the whole dynamic. It prevents the screening from becoming two urban professionals interpreting a rural crisis for an urban audience. The community leader's presence makes it a conversation, not just a presentation.

Logistically, make sure their travel and accommodation are covered and that they feel comfortable with the format. Being on a panel in Bogotá is a very different environment than being at home.

already on it. the filmmaker is covering travel costs and we're going to prep together beforehand so nobody feels ambushed by questions. I've learned the hard way that you can't just throw people in front of a microphone and expect it to go well even when they have the most important things to say

You've clearly thought this through — the preparation, the ethics, the logistics. Eight months of fieldwork and now careful planning for how the story gets told publicly. This sounds like a project that could genuinely shift how people understand what's happening in Chocó.

April 22 — mark it. I hope it gets the attention it deserves.

from your mouth to god's ears parcero. okay I need to try to sleep because I have a 7am meeting and it's almost 2am and I'm running on adrenaline and a nicotine patch. gracias por escuchar. buenas noches
Get some rest — you've earned the excitement. This sounds like something really special. Buenas noches, and congratulations again.
